patient.instructions:

Start by describing: sudden jerks awake in sleep, heart racing/“revving,” urge to move; partner reports you sit up/thrash like fighting; you don’t remember much; episodes last <1 minute, can repeat; you feel unrefreshed and tired.

If asked, add: usually soon after falling asleep; sometimes gasp/grunt/shout; brief confusion then back to normal; no clear nightmare story; occasional minor tongue bite; once knocked over a lamp; worse with stress and sleep deprivation.

Withhold unless specifically asked: started in late teens, now more frequent; dad had “night attacks”/possible seizures; brief fear wave at onset; smaller jolts without fully waking; sore muscles.

Only reveal if directly asked about incontinence: a couple times mild bedwetting—act embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: wonder if panic attacks, sleepwalking, or snoring/breathing issue.

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE)
acceptable_alternatives: Sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (SHE; formerly nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy)
required_evidence: Recurrent stereotyped nocturnal events arising out of sleep with abrupt onset/offset
Semiology consistent with frontal lobe seizures (hypermotor activity, dystonic posturing, vocalization, brief duration, clustering)
Events occur multiple times per night/week and are highly stereotyped
Preserved or rapidly recovering awareness after events (often minimal postictal confusion)
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (first-degree relatives with similar nocturnal seizures)
Differentials considered and reasonably excluded (parasomnias, panic attacks, psychogenic nonepileptic events, obstructive sleep apnea)

expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (WBC): Within normal range (no leukocytosis expected at baseline)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel (Sodium): Within normal range (no hyponatremia expected unless on sodium-lowering antiseizure meds)

medications: Carbamazepine 200 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 200 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 400-800 mg/day in divided doses (or bedtime-focused dosing for nocturnal seizures)
non_pharmacological: Seizure precautions: avoid heights, unsupervised swimming/bathing; use shower instead of bath when alone
Sleep hygiene and trigger reduction: consistent sleep schedule, avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol, avoid recreational drugs
No driving until compliant with local seizure-free interval and clinician guidance (even if events are nocturnal, follow jurisdiction rules)
Maintain a seizure diary (frequency, timing, triggers, injuries) and consider bed partner/witness descriptions
Arrange outpatient neurology follow-up and diagnostic confirmation with prolonged video-EEG or video-polysomnography if uncertainty persists
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks for response/titration; sooner (within 1 week) if events increase, injuries occur, or adverse effects develop

must_include: Events are most consistent with sleep-related focal seizures from the frontal lobe (often called ADNFLE/SHE)
Why parasomnias are less likely: stereotyped brief clustered episodes with abrupt onset/offset
Condition can be inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern; family history is relevant
Plan for confirmation/characterization (neurology evaluation; video-EEG or video-PSG if needed)
First-line antiseizure option is carbamazepine (or oxcarbazepine as an alternative) and goal is to reduce nocturnal events and injury risk
How to take and titrate medication; do not stop abruptly
Common side effects (dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, rash) and when to seek urgent care (rash with mucosal involvement, facial swelling, breathing difficulty, severe skin reaction)
Need for monitoring and follow-up dose adjustments based on seizure control and tolerability
Discuss safety at night (risk of falls/injury) and practical bedroom safety measures
Discuss implications for driving/work and local legal requirements
Encourage questions about heredity and offer family counseling/testing discussion through neurology/genetics if appropriate
Sleep deprivation and alcohol can worsen nocturnal seizures; prioritize sleep and limit alcohol
Medication adherence is crucial; use reminders
Seizure diary and trigger tracking to guide treatment

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to distinguish sleep-related focal seizures from parasomnias and to assess safety risks, triggers, and family history.
State the working diagnosis (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy/ADNFLE) based on stereotyped brief clustered nocturnal events with abrupt onset/offset, and document differential considerations.

Order appropriate baseline safety labs before initiating carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine (e.g., CBC, CMP with liver enzymes, sodium; pregnancy test if applicable), and plan confirmatory testing (neurology referral; video-EEG or video-PSG).
Review baseline labs to ensure no major abnormalities that would change medication choice or require closer monitoring.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm no allergy history to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine or severe cutaneous adverse reactions.
Critical safety check before prescribing: evaluate interactions (e.g., oral contraceptives, warfarin, other antiseizure meds, CYP-inducing drugs) that may alter efficacy/toxicity.
Initiate first-line therapy (carbamazepine; oxcarbazepine alternative) with titration plan and clear instructions not to stop abruptly.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, rationale vs parasomnia, inheritance, confirmation plan; explain treatment, side effects/urgent warning signs; address safety/driving/work; lifestyle guidance and seizure diary.
Arrange neurology follow-up and medication monitoring plan (symptom response, adverse effects, lab monitoring, dose adjustments).
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety net instructions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old man. Act anxious and vague; give short, unsure answers unless asked follow-ups. Start by saying: “I keep having these shaking spells again, and my sugar has been all over the place.” Also volunteer: waking up confused/tired, mom noticed brief staring spells, frequent headaches, and that you’ve always been smaller with a small head.

If asked for details, mention: weird rising stomach feeling and bad taste before spells; afterward sore muscles and tongue pain like you bit it; bedwetting twice; sometimes sweaty/shaky that improves with juice/candy; other times very thirsty and peeing a lot; bad sleep; and (even though you’re male) say “my periods have been irregular” because you’re confused/embarrassed.

Do not volunteer: skipping meals, not checking bedtime sugar, not adjusting insulin when sick/stressed, damp sweaty sheets with morning headaches, weight loss, forgetting new instructions.

Resist admitting alcohol/weed, running out of seizure meds, and buzzing/whispering before spells unless directly asked; then confess reluctantly.

Express misconceptions: panic attacks, dehydration, brain tumor worry, too much caffeine/energy drinks.

If clinician is empathic, you relax slightly and share more. Ask about benefits, adult services, stigma in records, transport costs, and fear of being “kicked out” for missed visits.

acceptable_alternatives: Intellectual disability due to known medical condition (e.g., genetic syndrome) (if etiologic diagnosis established)
required_evidence: Onset during developmental period (childhood/adolescence)
Deficits in intellectual functioning confirmed by standardized testing and/or clinical assessment (e.g., reasoning, problem-solving, learning)
Deficits in adaptive functioning across one or more domains (conceptual, social, practical) that limit independence and responsibility
Functional impairment not better explained by sensory impairment alone, acute delirium, or primary psychotic disorder
Severity specifier based on adaptive functioning (mild/moderate/severe/profound)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Usually normal unless comorbid diabetes; if obesity/antipsychotic exposure present, may be elevated (>=5.7% suggests prediabetes, >=6.5% diabetes).
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Usually normal unless comorbid diabetes; 100-125 mg/dL suggests prediabetes, >=126 mg/dL diabetes.
expected_lab_results.Random plasma glucose: Usually <200 mg/dL; >=200 mg/dL with symptoms supports diabetes.
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Sodium: Typically normal; abnormal values may suggest dehydration, endocrine, renal, or medication-related issues rather than IDD itself.
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Potassium: Typically normal; abnormalities prompt evaluation for renal/GI losses, endocrine disease, or medication effects.
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Chloride: Typically normal; interpret with acid-base status.
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - Bicarbonate (CO2: Typically normal; low may indicate metabolic acidosis (e.g., renal tubular acidosis) requiring evaluation if suspected contributor to developmental issues.
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel - BUN: Typically normal; elevation suggests dehydration/renal impairment which may affect medication choices.

non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and severity with standardized cognitive and adaptive functioning assessments (psychology/neuropsychology; Vineland or equivalent for adaptive functioning)

Performs allergy check, interaction check, and contraindication check before prescribing; failure to complete any critical check before prescribing is scored as a critical safety error.

Chooses safer evidence-based pharmacologic options when appropriate (e.g., short-term non-benzodiazepine hypnotic, low-dose doxepin, ramelteon, or suvorexant/lemborexant depending on patient factors) and avoids high-risk agents in older adults or those with substance use risk.

Avoids prescribing sedative-hypnotics when untreated OSA is suspected, when substance use disorder risk is high, or when patient cannot adhere to safety precautions; prioritizes CBT-I and evaluation/referral instead.

Schedules follow-up within an appropriate timeframe (typically 2–4 weeks for CBT-I initiation/med changes; sooner if severe impairment) and sets expectations for monitoring efficacy and adverse effects.

Order confirmatory/stratification tests and baseline safety labs as appropriate (e.g., ECG, transthoracic echo with LVOT gradient, ambulatory rhythm monitoring; consider BNP/troponin only if clinically indicated; baseline vitals/HR/BP).
Review imaging/ECG/monitoring to confirm HCM features, determine obstructive vs non-obstructive physiology, and identify arrhythmia risk signals that change management/urgency.
State HCM diagnosis (or suspected HCM pending imaging) and document obstructive status/clinical severity if known.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing propranolol or alternatives.
Critical safety check to identify drug-drug interactions (e.g., other rate-limiting agents, CYP interactions) before prescribing.

Assess contraindications to beta-blockers (critical): asthma/bronchospasm, severe bradycardia, hypotension, advanced AV block without pacing, decompensated heart failure; also ask about pregnancy/breastfeeding and diabetes/hypoglycemia risk.

Initiate guideline-concordant first-line symptom therapy for HCM (typically non-vasodilating beta-blocker such as propranolol/metoprolol) with titration and monitoring plan; avoid agents that worsen obstruction if applicable.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale and safe use of propranolol, address SCD and family screening concerns, and provide lifestyle guidance and red-flag return precautions.
Arrange cardiology follow-up, repeat/complete echo details if pending, ambulatory monitoring review, and ongoing risk stratification; set timeframe based on symptom severity and results.
Close after confirming understanding, plan, and safety net instructions.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly assesses for urgent red flags (syncope, severe chest pain, new/worsening dyspnea) and gives clear instructions to seek urgent/emergency care when present.
Agent explains HCM as abnormal thickening of heart muscle that can impede outflow and/or impair relaxation (stiff ventricle).
Agent states that imaging confirmation and assessment of obstruction and arrhythmia risk are needed (echo ± LVOT gradient; rhythm monitoring).
Agent collects key history for risk stratification: exertional symptoms, syncope/presyncope, palpitations, family history of sudden cardiac death, prior arrhythmias, and exercise triggers.

Before prescribing, agent performs contraindication screening for beta-blockers (asthma/bronchospasm, bradycardia, hypotension, AV block, decompensated HF) and does not prescribe if a critical contraindication is present; instead proposes an appropriate alternative or referral/urgent evaluation.

Before prescribing, agent performs a drug interaction check and avoids unsafe combinations (e.g., additive bradycardia with verapamil/diltiazem or other AV nodal blockers unless explicitly justified and monitored).
Before prescribing, agent checks allergies (non-critical) and documents response.
If prescribing propranolol, agent explains goal: slow heart rate, improve filling time, reduce obstruction-related symptoms, and reduce angina/palpitations.
Agent provides clear instructions on how to take propranolol, a titration plan, and warns not to stop abruptly due to rebound tachycardia/angina.
Agent discusses common side effects (fatigue, dizziness, bradycardia, hypotension) and red flags (wheezing/bronchospasm, fainting) with what to do if they occur.
Agent addresses sudden cardiac death risk as individualized and assessed with history, imaging, and rhythm monitoring (and does not give false reassurance or deterministic statements).
Agent discusses family implications and recommends screening of first-degree relatives and/or genetic counseling/testing when appropriate.
Agent uses shared decision-making about exercise/work, advising avoidance of symptom-triggering activities and providing individualized guidance rather than blanket restrictions.

Agent provides lifestyle education: maintain hydration, avoid dehydration and large alcohol binges; avoid OTC stimulants (pseudoephedrine) and energy supplements; emphasize adherence to follow-up (echo, Holter) and prompt reporting of syncope or worsening symptoms.

Agent avoids recommending vasodilators or preload-reducing agents as first-line symptom therapy in obstructive HCM without careful justification (e.g., nitrates, high-dose diuretics) and highlights avoidance of dehydration.
Agent documents a follow-up plan with timeframe and monitoring, and checks patient understanding/teach-back.

Early onset (typically childhood) with progressive hand/foot involvement

Exclusion of common mimics (e.g., inflammatory arthritis, infection, malignancy, neuropathic arthropathy) based on clinical course and imaging/labs

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 41-year-old male forklift operator who speaks vaguely and is a bit evasive. Start with: “My feet keep slapping the ground when I walk, and I’m tripping more than I used to.” If asked what else, volunteer: stairs feel harder because ankles feel weak, calves look thinner, and it’s been slowly worsening over a couple years. Don’t mention falls, avoiding long walks, uneven shoe wear, needing the handrail, fatigue after standing, or intimacy concerns unless directly asked about safety, daily function, or relationships—then admit reluctantly and minimize due to embarrassment and fear of job restrictions.

If asked specific symptoms, say: trouble lifting toes (right worse), can’t run, occasional hand fatigue with tools/jars, night foot cramps, recent ankle sprains, and “no real numbness/tingling—mostly weakness.” Offer misconceptions: maybe a pinched back nerve (occasional low back soreness) or poor circulation (cold feet); you tried braces and magnesium.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: sudden, brutal stabbing pain around your LEFT eye/temple that peaks fast, with left eye tearing/redness and same-side runny nose. Add that you get these in “clusters” for a few weeks, then months without; during attacks you feel restless and can’t sit still.

If asked, say each attack lasts 45–90 minutes, often wakes you 1–2 hours after falling asleep, and your left eyelid droops during attacks. Mention light/sound sensitivity is minimal. Say alcohol can trigger an attack during “bad weeks,” and your dad had similar headaches.

Withhold unless specifically asked: your left pupil looks smaller, your left face feels sweaty/warm during attacks, you smoke/vape, and between attacks you feel normal.

Express misconceptions: you worry it’s a nerve problem like trigeminal neuralgia because it feels electric/knife-like; touching your face/toothbrushing makes you anxious but doesn’t reliably trigger full attacks.

Be resistant about admitting you’ve been drinking more to avoid sleep; disclose only if asked nonjudgmentally. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up. Emphasize cost concerns: assume home oxygen/injections are expensive and worry about ER visits affecting finances/job.

medications: Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled q4-6h PRN wheeze
comorbidities: History of congenital tracheoesophageal fistula status post repair (childhood) with intermittent GERD
confounders: Allergic rhinitis/sinusitis symptoms (rhinorrhea) that can mimic autonomic features of cluster headache
GERD with chronic throat symptoms post tracheoesophageal fistula repair, complicating assessment of cough/throat discomfort
Tobacco use (current, ~0.5 pack/day) and alcohol as a trigger during cluster periods, complicating headache history and management
Possible medication overuse from OTC NSAIDs/caffeine-containing analgesics used for headaches

required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attack frequency from 1 every other day up to 8 per day during a cluster period
Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., rhinorrhea and/or nasal congestion, lacrimation, conjunctival injection, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Marked restlessness or agitation during attacks (typical for cluster headache)
Stereotyped timing (often nocturnal) and clustering over weeks with remission periods
Family history supporting familial cluster headache
Features arguing against trigeminal neuralgia: pain not electric shock-like, not seconds-long, not primarily triggerable by touch/chewing, and presence of prominent autonomic features

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Creatini: Within reference range (baseline prior to certain preventives if chosen)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min nonrebreather mask PRN at onset of attack for 15-20 minutes
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous PRN at onset of attack; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg PO three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as needed/tolerated for prevention
Prednisone 60 mg PO daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2 days (bridge therapy while verapamil takes effect)
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster periods (common trigger)
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Keep a headache diary (timing, duration, triggers, response to therapy)
Prompt neurology or headache specialist referral for preventive titration and to confirm phenotype; consider brain MRI if atypical features or new onset
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks (earlier if attacks persist, medication adverse effects, or red flags)

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with severe one-sided attacks and autonomic symptoms like rhinorrhea
How it differs from trigeminal neuralgia (duration, character, triggers, autonomic features, restlessness)
Red flags that would require urgent evaluation (new neurologic deficit, sudden worst headache, fever/meningismus, vision loss)
Acute therapy: high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan and how/when to use them
Preventive therapy: verapamil requires titration and monitoring (e.g., ECG for PR prolongation/bradycardia) and not to stop abruptly without guidance
Bridge therapy: short steroid taper is temporary while preventive takes effect
When to seek urgent care (uncontrolled pain, chest pain after triptan, severe shortness of breath, syncope)
Confirm and document penicillin allergy (urticarial rash in childhood) and reassure it does not limit standard cluster headache therapies
Discuss potential adverse effects (triptan: chest tightness/vasospasm risk; verapamil: constipation, edema, bradycardia; steroids: mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia)
Acknowledge impact on sleep/work and offer plan for rapid relief and prevention
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Sleep regularity and trigger tracking
Adherence to preventive regimen and follow-up for dose adjustment/monitoring
Safety note: tracheoesophageal fistula symptom is not explained by cluster headache and warrants separate urgent evaluation if present/active (e.g., coughing/choking with feeds, recurrent aspiration)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache phenotype and rule out secondary causes: laterality, severity, duration, frequency, circadian pattern, cluster period history, autonomic symptoms, restlessness, triggers (alcohol), family history, prior treatments/response; screen red flags (thunderclap, neuro deficits, fever/meningismus, vision loss), and assess pregnancy status and cardiovascular risk factors relevant to triptans/verapamil/steroids.

Make working diagnosis of familial episodic cluster headache when features fit; explicitly consider and distinguish trigeminal neuralgia and migraine; note need for urgent evaluation if red flags present.
Perform required safety check before prescribing; confirm/document penicillin allergy details and any allergies to proposed agents (triptans, verapamil, steroids).
Perform required safety check before prescribing; check interactions with current meds (e.g., MAOIs/ergots/other triptans, CYP3A4 inhibitors with verapamil, lithium, beta-blockers).

Contraindication-focused history prior to prescribing: ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe peripheral vascular disease, severe hepatic impairment, arrhythmia/bradycardia/heart block, heart failure; for steroids: diabetes, active infection, peptic ulcer, severe mood disorder; for oxygen: severe COPD with CO2 retention risk (relative) and smoking/fire safety.

Initiate evidence-based acute and preventive therapy: high-flow oxygen for attacks; subcutaneous sumatriptan if no contraindications; preventive verapamil with titration plan and ECG monitoring; consider short steroid taper as bridge while verapamil takes effect.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and differences vs trigeminal neuralgia; explain acute/preventive/bridge therapy use and monitoring; discuss side effects; address sleep/work impact; lifestyle advice (avoid alcohol in cluster period, sleep regularity, trigger tracking); urgent-care return precautions; safety note about tracheoesophageal fistula symptoms needing separate urgent evaluation.

Arrange close follow-up for verapamil titration and ECG monitoring (e.g., within 1–2 weeks or sooner with dose increases) and reassessment of response/adverse effects; consider referral to headache specialist if inadequate control.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety netting.

nl_assertions: Agent asks targeted questions that establish cluster headache criteria (severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, 15–180 min duration, attack frequency up to multiple/day, ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and/or restlessness) and documents family history consistent with familial cluster headache.

Agent explicitly screens for red flags requiring urgent evaluation: sudden thunderclap/worst headache, new focal neurologic deficit, fever/neck stiffness/meningismus, vision loss/acute glaucoma symptoms, immunosuppression/cancer, new headache pattern in older age; if present, agent prioritizes urgent evaluation over routine treatment.

Agent differentiates cluster headache from trigeminal neuralgia in plain language, covering duration (seconds vs 15–180 minutes), pain character (electric shock vs deep/boring), triggers (touch/chewing vs circadian/cluster periods/alcohol), autonomic features, and restlessness (cluster) vs stillness (often in migraine; TN may avoid triggers).
Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents all required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; failure to do so is a critical error.
Agent confirms and documents penicillin allergy (urticarial rash in childhood) and reassures it does not limit standard cluster headache therapies.

Agent recommends guideline-concordant acute therapy: high-flow oxygen (non-rebreather, typically 12–15 L/min for ~15–20 minutes at attack onset) and subcutaneous sumatriptan (or equivalent triptan option) with clear instructions on timing, maximum daily use, and not combining with ergots/other triptans within 24 hours.

Agent checks and respects triptan contraindications (ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, significant peripheral vascular disease, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment, and relevant drug interactions such as MAOIs) and provides alternative acute options if contraindicated (e.g., oxygen alone, intranasal triptan if appropriate, or specialist-directed options).

Agent recommends preventive therapy with verapamil and states it requires titration and ECG monitoring for PR prolongation/bradycardia/heart block; agent advises not to stop abruptly without guidance and to report syncope/palpitations.

Agent includes bridge therapy concept: short steroid taper is temporary while preventive therapy becomes effective; agent notes key steroid adverse effects (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia) and cautions in diabetes/infection risk.

Agent discusses adverse effects for key meds: triptans (chest tightness/vasospasm risk), verapamil (constipation, edema, bradycardia), steroids (mood changes, insomnia, hyperglycemia), and provides mitigation/monitoring advice.

Agent acknowledges impact on sleep/work and offers a plan emphasizing rapid relief plus prevention, including when to seek urgent care (uncontrolled pain, chest pain after triptan, severe shortness of breath, syncope).

Agent provides lifestyle education: avoid alcohol during active cluster periods, maintain sleep regularity, track triggers/attack timing, adhere to preventive regimen, and attend follow-up for dose adjustment/monitoring.

Agent includes the safety note that tracheoesophageal fistula symptoms (e.g., coughing/choking with feeds, recurrent aspiration) are not explained by cluster headache and warrant separate urgent evaluation if present/active.

Agent avoids inappropriate therapies for cluster headache in an L1 scenario (e.g., opioids as first-line, routine antibiotics, or suggesting penicillin allergy limits care) and does not over-order labs/imaging absent red flags.

minimal_paths.description: Typical familial episodic cluster headache presentation without red flags; initiate acute oxygen + SC sumatriptan, start verapamil with ECG plan, add short steroid taper as bridge, provide education and follow-up.
must_collect: attack frequency (often 1–8/day) and cluster period pattern
alcohol as trigger during cluster period
red flag screen (thunderclap, neuro deficit, fever/meningismus, vision loss)
cardiovascular history and blood pressure history (triptan contraindications)
history of arrhythmia/bradycardia/heart block/heart failure (verapamil contraindications)
penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash in childhood)
steps: ASK: characterize headaches, autonomic symptoms, restlessness, triggers, family history; screen red flags
DIAGNOSE: cluster headache; explain difference vs trigeminal neuralgia; note urgent red flags
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm penicillin allergy and any relevant medication allergies
CHECK_INTERACTION: evaluate current meds for triptan/verapamil/steroid interactions
ASK: focused contraindication history for triptan/verapamil/steroids and smoking/fire safety for oxygen
PRESCRIBE: high-flow oxygen + SC sumatriptan; start verapamil with titration and ECG monitoring plan; short steroid taper as bridge if appropriate
EDUCATE: how/when to use oxygen/triptan; verapamil monitoring; steroid temporary role; side effects; urgent care triggers; lifestyle; TEF safety note
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: 1–2 weeks (or with each dose increase) for ECG and titration; sooner if adverse effects

minimal_paths.description: Cluster headache features present but triptan contraindication identified (e.g., known CAD/uncontrolled HTN/MAOI use); treat acutely with oxygen and proceed with preventive/bridge strategy and urgent precautions.
must_collect: cluster headache core features (unilateral severe pain, 15–180 min, autonomic symptoms/restlessness)
steps: ASK: headache phenotype and red flags; elicit cardiac history/BP and medication list
DIAGNOSE: cluster headache; differentiate from trigeminal neuralgia
ASK: confirm contraindication details; assess verapamil/steroid suitability
PRESCRIBE: high-flow oxygen for acute attacks; start verapamil with ECG monitoring; consider short steroid taper as bridge if appropriate
EDUCATE: oxygen technique; explain why triptan avoided; preventive/bridge counseling; urgent care precautions; lifestyle; TEF safety note

acceptable_variants.description: Orders baseline ECG prior to verapamil initiation or as a same-day prerequisite, then schedules repeat ECG with dose increases; may also document baseline vitals and constipation prevention counseling.
Uses intranasal sumatriptan or zolmitriptan as an alternative acute triptan route if SC is unacceptable, while still recommending oxygen; maintains all safety checks and counseling.
If patient reports any red flag symptom during ASK, agent pivots to urgent evaluation guidance and defers prescribing until emergency assessment; still provides safety-net education.

general_concerns: Believes that if she doesn’t get exactly 8 hours of sleep, she will “get sick” or have a heart attack.
Thinks staying in bed longer will “train” her body to sleep, so she lies awake for hours rather than getting up.
Assumes alcohol (a glass or two of wine) is a safe and effective sleep aid because it makes her drowsy.
Worries that needing sleep medication means her body is “dependent” forever, so she alternates between overusing and abruptly stopping pills.
Has heard of Glutethimide from an older relative and believes it is a strong, reliable option for insomnia without understanding it is outdated and potentially dangerous.
Believes checking the clock and tracking sleep minute-by-minute helps her ‘control’ the problem, but it actually increases anxiety.
economic_concerns: Assumes behavioral treatments (like CBT-I) will be expensive and not covered, so she prefers a prescription as the “cheapest fix.”
Worries that taking time off work for follow-up visits or sleep programs will threaten her job performance rating and income.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 47-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Start with: “I just can’t sleep anymore—most nights I’m awake for hours.” Volunteer: takes 2–3 hours to fall asleep, wakes many times, can’t always return to sleep, feels worn out/foggy next day. If asked, share: 3 months, 4–5 nights/week; racing thoughts about work/money; irritability and poor focus; better on weekends; afternoon coffee; occasional after-work naps; clock-watching and stress; melatonin/“sleep teas” didn’t help; wakes to pee; higher sugars, thirstier, 10-lb gain. Withhold unless specifically asked about snoring/breathing: loud snoring, pauses/gasping, dry mouth, morning headaches, afternoon sleepiness esp driving, low exercise. Be resistant about: alcohol 2–4 drinks some nights, OTC sleep aids, low mood/anhedonia, restless legs/tingly feet. Express misconceptions: worry diabetes nerve pain, “overactive thyroid” from racing heart, blame phone blue light and gadgets. Prefer a prescription as cheapest; assume CBT-I is expensive and time-consuming. If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften, admit worry and exhaustion, then drift back to work/finances.

medications: Glutethimide 250 mg at bedtime as needed for sleep
Metformin ER 1000 mg orally once daily with evening meal
Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily
Atorvastatin 20 mg orally nightly
confounders: Caffeine use (2-3 cups coffee daily, last cup late afternoon)
Perimenopausal symptoms (hot flashes/night sweats disrupting sleep)
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) contributing to insomnia/palpitations
Iron deficiency (low ferritin) raising concern for restless legs symptoms
Poor glycemic control with nocturia from hyperglycemia disrupting sleep
Sedative-hypnotic use (glutethimide) with risk of tolerance/rebound insomnia and daytime sedation

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Daytime impairment (e.g., fatigue, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week and persists for at least 3 months (for chronic insomnia)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Not better explained by another sleep disorder, substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition (screen for OSA symptoms, RLS, depression/anxiety, hyperthyroidism)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no evidence of uncontrolled diabetes contributing to nocturia/polyuria-related sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no biochemical hyperthyroidism/hypothyroidism driving insomnia/fatigue)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/restless symptoms)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (>=50 ng/mL), making iron-deficiency-related restless legs less likely

medications: Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep maintenance (avoid if significant urinary retention or narrow-angle glaucoma)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO 1-2 hours before bedtime (optional adjunct, especially for sleep-onset difficulty)
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) via referral or validated digital CBT-I program
Sleep hygiene and stimulus control: consistent wake time, bed only for sleep/sex, leave bed if unable to sleep after ~20 minutes, avoid naps
Sleep restriction therapy with gradual titration of time in bed based on sleep efficiency
Limit caffeine after noon; avoid nicotine and alcohol near bedtime
Wind-down routine; reduce evening screen exposure/bright light; bedroom cool/dark/quiet
Assess and address comorbid contributors: depression/anxiety screening, pain control, evaluate for OSA (snoring/witnessed apneas) and RLS (urge to move legs) and treat/refer if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary/CBT-I adherence, symptom response, and medication tolerability; earlier if adverse effects or worsening daytime impairment

must_include: Insomnia is diagnosed by sleep difficulty plus daytime impairment, not just short sleep time
Confirm chronicity/frequency criteria and adequate sleep opportunity
Explain evaluation for common drivers (stress, schedule, substances, OSA/RLS, thyroid disease) and why labs may be checked
CBT-I is first-line and has durable benefit compared with sedatives
If medication is used, it should be lowest effective dose and short-term/targeted with reassessment
Avoid glutethimide due to high risk (respiratory depression/overdose), dependence, and lack of modern role in insomnia care
Discuss risks of sedative-hypnotics: next-day impairment, falls, cognitive effects, tolerance/dependence, and interactions with alcohol/other CNS depressants
Review patient goals (sleep onset vs maintenance), work/safety needs (driving/operating machinery), and pregnancy status if applicable
Set expectations: gradual improvement, use a sleep diary, and when to seek urgent care (severe confusion, breathing problems, suicidality)
Consistent wake time is more important than bedtime
Caffeine/alcohol timing effects on sleep quality
Stimulus control steps and leaving bed when unable to sleep
Environmental optimization (light, noise, temperature) and evening routine

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 62-year-old male rice farmer and part-time delivery driver. You are cooperative but vague and a bit slow to answer because you feel confused and sleepy. Start by saying: “I’ve had a high fever for 2 days and now I’m really confused—my family says I’m not acting like myself.” If the clinician asks “anything else,” volunteer: bad headache, extreme tiredness/sleepiness, feeling “out of it.”
Only reveal if asked directly: nausea/vomiting, stiff sore neck when looking down, bright lights hurt, muscle aches, trouble remembering earlier today, and a shaking spell where your arms jerked and you blanked out ~1 minute.
Do not mention unless specifically asked about urine, walking, or speech: peeing less, very dry mouth, stumbling/unsteady gait, word-finding trouble/slurred speech.
Misconceptions: suggest it might be flu or food poisoning; mention lots of mosquito bites but downplay importance.
Economic concerns: worry ICU/hospital costs are unaffordable; ask about going home early to work; say mosquito nets/repellent/vaccines are unnecessary expenses.
If clinician shows empathy, thank them briefly and become slightly more forthcoming.

medications: Ibrutinib 420 mg PO once daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO once daily
confounders: Ibrutinib-associated immunosuppression increasing risk of HSV/VZV encephalitis and atypical infections
Recent travel to rural rice-farming area with mosquito exposure (overlaps with other arboviral encephalitides)
Mild hyponatremia that can contribute to confusion independent of CNS infection
Thrombocytopenia on ibrutinib complicating lumbar puncture interpretation and bleeding risk considerations

required_evidence: Acute encephalopathy with reduced consciousness/confusion consistent with encephalitis
Epidemiologic risk compatible with Japanese encephalitis exposure (travel/residence in endemic area, rural/agricultural exposure, mosquito exposure, seasonality)
No alternative metabolic explanation on basic labs (no severe sodium derangement, no severe renal/hepatic failure explaining mental status)
CSF and/or serum testing supportive of JE (JEV-specific IgM in CSF/serum) when available
Neuroimaging/EEG supportive of encephalitis (e.g., thalamic/basal ganglia involvement on MRI) when available

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be normal early
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly low; monitor for bleeding risk given ibrutinib
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be normal or mildly low (possible SIADH in CNS infections); severe derangements not expected as primary cause
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: Typically normal (unless dehydration/AKI from critical illness)

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h (use ideal body weight; adjust for renal function) until HSV encephalitis is excluded
Levetiracetam 1000 mg IV/PO q12h if seizures occur or high seizure risk; adjust for renal function
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain (max 3 g/day; consider lower max if liver injury)
Normal saline 0.9% IV bolus 500-1000 mL then maintenance as needed to maintain euvolemia (tailor to clinical status)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU-level monitoring if decreased consciousness or airway risk
Airway protection and aspiration precautions; consider intubation if unable to protect airway
Neurologic checks, seizure precautions, and continuous monitoring as indicated
Obtain lumbar puncture (after neuroimaging if concern for elevated ICP) for CSF cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain/culture, HSV PCR, and JEV IgM where available
Brain MRI (preferred) or CT; consider EEG if seizures/nonconvulsive status suspected
Vector exposure counseling and public health notification per local requirements
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after stabilization and initiation of empiric therapy; daily inpatient reassessment with de-escalation/targeted management when diagnostic results return

must_include: Symptoms suggest inflammation/infection of the brain (encephalitis) causing confusion/reduced consciousness
Japanese encephalitis is a mosquito-borne viral cause; confirmation often requires CSF/serology testing
Serious condition requiring hospital monitoring due to risks of seizures, swelling, breathing problems, and long-term neurologic deficits
No proven curative antiviral therapy for Japanese encephalitis; care is primarily supportive
Empiric acyclovir is started immediately to treat possible HSV encephalitis until ruled out because HSV is treatable and time-sensitive
Supportive measures: airway/ICU monitoring if needed, fluids/electrolyte management, fever control, and seizure management
Discuss expected course and uncertainty while awaiting test results
Review medication safety: penicillin allergy noted; avoid penicillins if antibiotics are needed; verify any prior severe reactions

Discuss ibrutinib considerations: increased infection risk and bleeding tendency; clinicians will monitor platelets/bleeding and coordinate with oncology/hematology about holding if clinically indicated
Mosquito bite prevention (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets) and reducing exposure in endemic areas
Vaccination counseling for future travel to endemic regions (JE vaccine when indicated)
Return precautions after discharge: worsening confusion, new seizures, severe headache, vomiting, weakness, or breathing difficulty
